prompt.name: pattern-copy

content.thinking: Let me start by understanding the project structure and the real app to build a proper Storybook setup.

input.prompt:
Thoroughly explore this codebase to understand the full runtime environment. I need to know:

1. Read src/main.tsx, src/App.tsx, and follow ALL imports to understand:
   - The full provider tree
   - All CSS imports (especially root-level ones)
   - All routing setup
   - All context providers

2. Find ALL pages/routes and their components - read the actual page files to understand what they render

3. Find ALL context providers and what they provide

4. Find ALL hooks that do data fetching (fetch, axios, etc.) - what URLs/endpoints they hit

5. Find ALL hooks/code that reads browser state (localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies, URL params)

6. Find ALL portal usage (createPortal, modal roots, etc.)

7. Find ALL reusable UI components (buttons, cards, inputs, etc.)

8. Read package.json for dependencies

9. Read .storybook/main.ts and .storybook/preview.tsx if they exist

10. Check for any existing story files

Be very thorough - read every relevant file completely. I need the full picture of this app's runtime environment.

     6→        description: "Starting an Instagram blog is relatively easy and can be done in a few simple steps and some creativity as well",
     7→        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
     8→        datetime: "2023-03-16",
     9→        imageUrl: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1600096194534-95cf5ece04cf?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80",

    12→            name: 'Roel Aufderehar',
    13→            imageUrl: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
    14→        },
    15→        blogPost: `In today's digital age, social media has become an integral part of our lives, and Instagram, with its visually captivating platform, stands out as a popular choice for content creators looking to start a blog. Whether you're passionate about fashion, travel, food, fitness, or any other niche, Instagram offers an excellent opportunity to showcase your creativity and engage with a global audience. So, if you're eager to share your passions with the world, here's a step-by-step guide on how to start an Instagram blog and make it a resounding success.

1. Define Your Niche:
Before diving headfirst into Instagram blogging, it's crucial to identify your niche. Determine what interests you the most and where your expertise lies. A well-defined niche helps you stand out in a sea of content creators and attracts a dedicated audience genuinely interested in your content.

2. Create a Compelling Instagram Handle:
Your Instagram handle is essentially your brand name, so make it catchy and reflective of your niche. Keep it simple, memorable, and easy to spell to ensure that potential followers can find and remember you easily.

3. Develop a Cohesive Aesthetic:
Instagram is a visual platform, and maintaining a consistent and attractive aesthetic is key to grabbing attention and retaining followers. Choose a color palette, editing style, and overall theme that aligns with your niche and reflects your personality.

4. Craft an Engaging Bio:
Your Instagram bio is your chance to make a great first impression. Use it to concisely describe who you are, what your blog is about, and what value you provide to your followers. Include relevant keywords and a clear call-to-action (CTA) to encourage visitors to follow you.

5. Share High-Quality Content:
Quality is paramount on Instagram. Invest in a good camera or use your smartphone's advanced camera features to capture stunning images. Ensure your photos are well-lit, well-composed, and visually appealing. Additionally, use engaging captions that provide context, ask questions, or encourage interaction.

6. Plan Your Content Strategy:
Consistency is key to Instagram success. Plan your content in advance using content calendars or scheduling tools. This will help you maintain a steady flow of posts and ensure that you cover a diverse range of topics within your niche.

7. Utilize Instagram Stories and IGTV:
Instagram Stories and IGTV are powerful tools for connecting with your audience in a more authentic and engaging way. Use Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and announcements. IGTV allows for longer-form content, giving you an opportunity to showcase in-depth tutorials, travel vlogs, or interviews.

8. Engage with Your Audience:
Interact with your followers regularly by responding to comments, answering direct messages, and acknowledging user-generated content (UGC). Building a strong relationship with your audience fosters loyalty and encourages them to support your blog in the long run.

9. Collaborate and Network:
Collaborations with other content creators, brands, or influencers can significantly boost your exposure. Look for like-minded individuals or companies within your niche and explore partnership opportunities that align with your brand values.

10. Monitor Analytics and Adapt:
Keep a close eye on Instagram Insights to analyze the performance of your posts, identify the best times to post, and understand your audience demographics. Use this data to adapt your content strategy and optimize your Instagram blog for better engagement and growth.

Starting an Instagram blog may seem overwhelming, but with passion, dedication, and a well-thought-out approach, success is within reach. Remember, it's not just about followers and likes; it's about creating meaningful connections and sharing your unique voice with the world. So, take the leap, stay true to yourself, and watch your Instagram blog thrive!`

    33→        blogPost: `
    34→Instagram is a powerful platform for engaging with your audience, but knowing what to post can sometimes be challenging. To help you keep your feed interesting and your followers engaged, we've put together a list of content ideas that you can use to make your Instagram shine!


    35→1. Behind-the-Scenes:

    36→Take your followers behind the scenes and show them the process of creating your products or services. People love to see the human side of your brand and get a glimpse of what goes on behind closed doors.


    37→2. User-Generated Content (UGC):

    38→Encourage your followers to create content featuring your products or services and share it on their own profiles. UGC not only gives your brand credibility but also helps foster a sense of community among your followers.


    39→3. Inspirational Quotes:

    40→Share motivational and inspirational quotes that align with your brand values. Quotes are highly shareable and can resonate with your audience on a personal level.


    41→4. Product or Service Highlights:

    42→Highlight your best-selling products or most popular services. Showcase their features and benefits to entice your audience to make a purchase.


    43→5. Tutorials and How-Tos:

    44→Educate your followers with step-by-step tutorials and how-to guides related to your industry. Providing value through educational content can establish your brand as an authority in your niche.


    45→6. Customer Testimonials:

    46→Share positive feedback and testimonials from satisfied customers. Testimonials build trust and social proof, which can encourage others to do business with you.


    47→7. Polls and Surveys:

    48→Engage your audience by running polls and surveys in your Instagram Stories. This not only encourages interaction but also helps you gather valuable insights from your followers.


    49→8. Throwback Posts:

    50→Take a trip down memory lane and share old photos or milestones from your brand's history. Nostalgic content can evoke positive emotions and create a sense of nostalgia among your audience.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a complete picture. Let me create a comprehensive summary document:

## Complete Runtime Environment Analysis for Edgy Project

### 1. PROVIDER TREE & ROOT-LEVEL SETUP

**Entry Point:** `/src/index.jsx`
- Creates React root with ReactDOM
- Imports core UI CSS with Tailwind initialization
- No context providers in the app tree
- **CSS Root:** `/src/core-ui/index.css`
  - Imports Tailwind CSS
  - CSS variables: `--primary-font-color` (rgb(147, 157, 250)), `--primary-font-color-hover` (rgb(82, 98, 245))

**App Component Structure (`/src/App.jsx`):**
- Wraps everything in React Router (BrowserRouter)
- Navigation component (header) + Routes + Footer layout
- NO Context API providers - all state is in App component
- State management directly in App.jsx with useState hooks

### 2. FULL PROVIDER TREE

```
Root (index.jsx)
  └─ App.jsx
      └─ BrowserRouter (React Router)
          ├─ Navigation (header with logged-in state)
          ├─ Routes (13 routes total)
          └─ Footer
```

**NO context providers found** - The app uses component prop drilling for state:
- `loggedIn` (boolean)
- `user` (object with id, email, fullname, gender, plan, createdTime, password)
- `setLoggedIn` (setter)
- `setTriggeredLogout` (setter)
- `retrieveDatabase` (async function)
- `updateUser` (async function)
- `deleteUser` (function)
- `registerUser` (async function)

### 3. ALL ROUTES (React Router Setup)

**Routes in App.jsx:**
1. `/` → Landing
2. `/contact` → Contact
3. `/pricing` → Pricing
4. `/faq` → Faq
5. `/blog` → Blog
6. `/blog/:name` → BlogPost (dynamic)
7. `/about` → About
8. `/terms` → Terms
9. `/refunds` → Refunds
10. `/privacy` → Privacy
11. `/jobs` → Jobs
12. `/sign-in` → SignIn OR Profile (if logged in)
13. `/sign-up` → SignUp OR Profile (if logged in)
14. `/profile` → Profile (protected route, shows NotFound if not logged in)
15. `*` → NotFound (catch-all)

### 4. ALL PAGE/ROUTE COMPONENTS

**Landing Page (`/src/routes/landing/Landing.jsx`):**
- Renders: Hero, Partners, Feature, Cta, Stats, Pricing, Testimonials, Team, Comparison, Newsletter

**Sign In (`/src/routes/sign-in/`):**
- SignIn.jsx - Layout wrapper
- SignInForm.jsx - Form component with ReCAPTCHA validation

**Sign Up (`/src/routes/sign-up/`):**
- SignUp.jsx - Layout wrapper
- SignUpForm.jsx - Form component with ReCAPTCHA validation

**User Profile (`/src/routes/user-profile/Profile.jsx`):**
- Editable fields: fullname, email, gender, plan, password
- Delete account modal
- Change password modal (inline using fixed positioning)

**Content Pages:**
- Contact.jsx → ContactFormSection.jsx, ContactImg.jsx, CareerCta.jsx
- Pricing.jsx → PricingComparisonTable, PricingFaq, Partners
- Blog.jsx → BlogGrid, Newsletter
- BlogPost.jsx → Dynamic blog post viewer (finds post by window.location.pathname)
- About.jsx → AboutOne, AboutTwo, AboutThree, Team, Cta
- Faq.jsx → FaqList, Cta
- Jobs.jsx → JobsListing, JobsForm
- Terms.jsx → Static legal text
- Privacy.jsx → Static legal text
- Refunds.jsx → Static legal text

### 5. ALL CONTEXT PROVIDERS

**NONE** - This app does NOT use React Context API. All state is:
- In App.jsx root component
- Passed down as props
- Managed with useState/useEffect only

### 6. ALL HOOKS DOING DATA FETCHING

**Fetch operations in the codebase:**

**App.jsx:**
- `fetch(import.meta.env.VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL)` → GET user database
- `fetch(import.meta.env.VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL, { method: "POST" })` → UPDATE user data
- Uses Airtable SDK: `edgyBase("users").create()` / `edgyBase("users").destroy()`

**SignInForm.jsx:**
- `fetch(serverlUrl, { method: "POST" })` → ReCAPTCHA verification

**SignUpForm.jsx:**
- `fetch(serverlUrl, { method: "POST" })` → ReCAPTCHA verification

**ContactForm.jsx:**
- `fetch(serverlUrl, { method: "POST" })` → ReCAPTCHA verification

**Airtable Integration (`/src/airtable/airtable.js`):**
- Initializes Airtable base with API key and base ID from env vars

### 7. ALL BROWSER STATE READS (localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies, URL params)

**sessionStorage (in App.jsx):**
- `sessionStorage.getItem("loggedIn")` → Check login status
- `sessionStorage.getItem("user")` → Retrieve user JSON data
- `sessionStorage.setItem("loggedIn", "true")` → Set login state
- `sessionStorage.setItem("user", JSON.stringify(userData))` → Store user data
- `sessionStorage.removeItem("loggedIn")` → Clear login
- `sessionStorage.removeItem("user")` → Clear user data

**URL Parameters (in BlogPost.jsx):**
- `window.location.pathname` → Get blog post URL to find corresponding post from blogPostsData

**Window methods:**
- `window.scrollTo(0, 0)` → Scroll to top (used in multiple form submissions and navigation)

### 8. PORTAL USAGE & MODALS

**NO createPortal usage found** - All modals are inline conditional rendering using fixed positioning:

**Profile.jsx - Two modals using inline conditional rendering:**
1. Delete account confirmation dialog:
   - `deleteRequest` state trigger
   - Fixed overlay: `fixed inset-0 z-10 flex items-center justify-center overflow-y-auto bg-gray-900/80 p-4`

2. Change password modal:
   - `editPassword` state trigger
   - Fixed overlay: `fixed inset-0 z-10 flex items-center justify-center overflow-y-auto bg-gray-900/80 p-4`

### 9. ALL REUSABLE UI COMPONENTS

**UI Components:**

Core Reusable:
- `Button` (`/src/stories/Button.jsx`) - Storybook example component with size and color props
- Form elements: input, textarea, select (inline, Tailwind styled)
- Spinner/Loading indicator (SVG animation)

Layout Components:
- Navigation.jsx - Top navigation with responsive mobile/desktop
- Footer.jsx - Multi-column footer with social links and contact info

Feature Components:
- Cta.jsx - Call-to-action section
- Newsletter.jsx - Email signup form
- Team.jsx - Team member grid display
- Feature.jsx - Feature cards grid
- Testimonials.jsx - Testimonial cards
- Stats.jsx - Statistics display
- Comparison.jsx - Comparison table
- Partners.jsx - Partner logos

Form Components:
- SignInForm.jsx - Login form with ReCAPTCHA

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a complete picture. Let me create a comprehensive summary document:

## Complete Runtime Environment Analysis for Edgy Project

### 1. PROVIDER TREE & ROOT-LEVEL SETUP

**Entry Point:** `/src/index.jsx`
- Creates React root with ReactDOM
- Imports core UI CSS with Tailwind initialization
- No context providers in the app tree
- **CSS Root:** `/src/core-ui/index.css`
  - Imports Tailwind CSS
  - CSS variables: `--primary-font-color` (rgb(147, 157, 250)), `--primary-font-color-hover` (rgb(82, 98, 245))

**App Component Structure (`/src/App.jsx`):**
- Wraps everything in React Router (BrowserRouter)
- Navigation component (header) + Routes + Footer layout
- NO Context API providers - all state is in App component
- State management directly in App.jsx with useState hooks

### 2. FULL PROVIDER TREE

```
Root (index.jsx)
  └─ App.jsx
      └─ BrowserRouter (React Router)
          ├─ Navigation (header with logged-in state)
          ├─ Routes (13 routes total)
          └─ Footer
```

**NO context providers found** - The app uses component prop drilling for state:
- `loggedIn` (boolean)
- `user` (object with id, email, fullname, gender, plan, createdTime, password)
- `setLoggedIn` (setter)
- `setTriggeredLogout` (setter)
- `retrieveDatabase` (async function)
- `updateUser` (async function)
- `deleteUser` (function)
- `registerUser` (async function)

### 3. ALL ROUTES (React Router Setup)

**Routes in App.jsx:**
1. `/` → Landing
2. `/contact` → Contact
3. `/pricing` → Pricing
4. `/faq` → Faq
5. `/blog` → Blog
6. `/blog/:name` → BlogPost (dynamic)
7. `/about` → About
8. `/terms` → Terms
9. `/refunds` → Refunds
10. `/privacy` → Privacy
11. `/jobs` → Jobs
12. `/sign-in` → SignIn OR Profile (if logged in)
13. `/sign-up` → SignUp OR Profile (if logged in)
14. `/profile` → Profile (protected route, shows NotFound if not logged in)
15. `*` → NotFound (catch-all)

### 4. ALL PAGE/ROUTE COMPONENTS

**Landing Page (`/src/routes/landing/Landing.jsx`):**
- Renders: Hero, Partners, Feature, Cta, Stats, Pricing, Testimonials, Team, Comparison, Newsletter

**Sign In (`/src/routes/sign-in/`):**
- SignIn.jsx - Layout wrapper
- SignInForm.jsx - Form component with ReCAPTCHA validation

**Sign Up (`/src/routes/sign-up/`):**
- SignUp.jsx - Layout wrapper
- SignUpForm.jsx - Form component with ReCAPTCHA validation

**User Profile (`/src/routes/user-profile/Profile.jsx`):**
- Editable fields: fullname, email, gender, plan, password
- Delete account modal
- Change password modal (inline using fixed positioning)

**Content Pages:**
- Contact.jsx → ContactFormSection.jsx, ContactImg.jsx, CareerCta.jsx
- Pricing.jsx → PricingComparisonTable, PricingFaq, Partners
- Blog.jsx → BlogGrid, Newsletter
- BlogPost.jsx → Dynamic blog post viewer (finds post by window.location.pathname)
- About.jsx → AboutOne, AboutTwo, AboutThree, Team, Cta
- Faq.jsx → FaqList, Cta
- Jobs.jsx → JobsListing, JobsForm
- Terms.jsx → Static legal text
- Privacy.jsx → Static legal text
- Refunds.jsx → Static legal text

### 5. ALL CONTEXT PROVIDERS

**NONE** - This app does NOT use React Context API. All state is:
- In App.jsx root component
- Passed down as props
- Managed with useState/useEffect only

### 6. ALL HOOKS DOING DATA FETCHING

**Fetch operations in the codebase:**

**App.jsx:**
- `fetch(import.meta.env.VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL)` → GET user database
- `fetch(import.meta.env.VITE_AIRTABLE_SERVER_URL, { method: "POST" })` → UPDATE user data
- Uses Airtable SDK: `edgyBase("users").create()` / `edgyBase("users").destroy()`

**SignInForm.jsx:**
- `fetch(serverlUrl, { method: "POST" })` → ReCAPTCHA verification

**SignUpForm.jsx:**
- `fetch(serverlUrl, { method: "POST" })` → ReCAPTCHA verification

**ContactForm.jsx:**
- `fetch(serverlUrl, { method: "POST" })` → ReCAPTCHA verification

**Airtable Integration (`/src/airtable/airtable.js`):**
- Initializes Airtable base with API key and base ID from env vars

### 7. ALL BROWSER STATE READS (localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies, URL params)

**sessionStorage (in App.jsx):**
- `sessionStorage.getItem("loggedIn")` → Check login status
- `sessionStorage.getItem("user")` → Retrieve user JSON data
- `sessionStorage.setItem("loggedIn", "true")` → Set login state
- `sessionStorage.setItem("user", JSON.stringify(userData))` → Store user data
- `sessionStorage.removeItem("loggedIn")` → Clear login
- `sessionStorage.removeItem("user")` → Clear user data

**URL Parameters (in BlogPost.jsx):**
- `window.location.pathname` → Get blog post URL to find corresponding post from blogPostsData

**Window methods:**
- `window.scrollTo(0, 0)` → Scroll to top (used in multiple form submissions and navigation)

### 8. PORTAL USAGE & MODALS

**NO createPortal usage found** - All modals are inline conditional rendering using fixed positioning:

**Profile.jsx - Two modals using inline conditional rendering:**
1. Delete account confirmation dialog:
   - `deleteRequest` state trigger
   - Fixed overlay: `fixed inset-0 z-10 flex items-center justify-center overflow-y-auto bg-gray-900/80 p-4`

2. Change password modal:
   - `editPassword` state trigger
   - Fixed overlay: `fixed inset-0 z-10 flex items-center justify-center overflow-y-auto bg-gray-900/80 p-4`

### 9. ALL REUSABLE UI COMPONENTS

**UI Components:**

Core Reusable:
- `Button` (`/src/stories/Button.jsx`) - Storybook example component with size and color props
- Form elements: input, textarea, select (inline, Tailwind styled)
- Spinner/Loading indicator (SVG animation)

Layout Components:
- Navigation.jsx - Top navigation with responsive mobile/desktop
- Footer.jsx - Multi-column footer with social links and contact info

Feature Components:
- Cta.jsx - Call-to-action section
- Newsletter.jsx - Email signup form
- Team.jsx - Team member grid display
- Feature.jsx - Feature cards grid
- Testimonials.jsx - Testimonial cards
- Stats.jsx - Statistics display
- Comparison.jsx - Comparison table
- Partners.jsx - Partner logos

- BlogGrid.jsx - Blog post list

- FaqList.jsx - FAQ accordion
- PricingComparisonTable.jsx - Pricing table
- PricingFaq.jsx - Pricing-specific FAQ

1→
     2→const blogPostsData = [
     3→    {
     4→        title: "How to Start an Instagram Blog",
     5→        href: "/blog/how-to-start-an-instagram-blog",
     6→        description: "Starting an Instagram blog is relatively easy and can be done in a few simple steps and some creativity as well",
     7→        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
     8→        datetime: "2023-03-16",
     9→        imageUrl: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1600096194534-95cf5ece04cf?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80",
    10→        readingTime: '5 min',
    11→        author: {
    12→            name: 'Roel Aufderehar',
    13→            imageUrl: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
    14→        },
    15→        blogPost: `In today's digital age, social media has become an integral part of our lives, and Instagram, with its visually captivating platform, stands out as a popular choice for content creators looking to start a blog. Whether you're passionate about fashion, travel, food, fitness, or any other niche, Instagram offers an excellent opportunity to showcase your creativity and engage with a global audience. So, if you're eager to share your passions with the world, here's a step-by-step guide on how to start an Instagram blog and make it a resounding success.\n\n1. Define Your Niche:\nBefore diving headfirst into Instagram blogging, it's crucial to identify your niche. Determine what interests you the most and where your expertise lies. A well-defined niche helps you stand out in a sea of content creators and attracts a dedicated audience genuinely interested in your content.\n\n2. Create a Compelling Instagram Handle:\nYour Instagram handle is essentially your brand name, so make it catchy and reflective of your niche. Keep it simple, memorable, and easy to spell to ensure that potential followers can find and remember you easily.\n\n3. Develop a Cohesive Aesthetic:\nInstagram is a visual platform, and maintaining a consistent and attractive aesthetic is key to grabbing attention and retaining followers. Choose a color palette, editing style, and overall theme that aligns with your niche and reflects your personality.\n\n4. Craft an Engaging Bio:\nYour Instagram bio is your chance to make a great first impression. Use it to concisely describe who you are, what your blog is about, and what value you provide to your followers. Include relevant keywords and a clear call-to-action (CTA) to encourage visitors to follow you.\n\n5. Share High-Quality Content:\nQuality is paramount on Instagram. Invest in a good camera or use your smartphone's advanced camera features to capture stunning images. Ensure your photos are well-lit, well-composed, and visually appealing. Additionally, use engaging captions that provide context, ask questions, or encourage interaction.\n\n6. Plan Your Content Strategy:\nConsistency is key to Instagram success. Plan your content in advance using content calendars or scheduling tools. This will help you maintain a steady flow of posts and ensure that you cover a diverse range of topics within your niche.\n\n7. Utilize Instagram Stories and IGTV:\nInstagram Stories and IGTV are powerful tools for connecting with your audience in a more authentic and engaging way. Use Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and announcements. IGTV allows for longer-form content, giving you an opportunity to showcase in-depth tutorials, travel vlogs, or interviews.\n\n8. Engage with Your Audience:\nInteract with your followers regularly by responding to comments, answering direct messages, and acknowledging user-generated content (UGC). Building a strong relationship with your audience fosters loyalty and encourages them to support your blog in the long run.\n\n9. Collaborate and Network:\nCollaborations with other content creators, brands, or influencers can significantly boost your exposure. Look for like-minded individuals or companies within your niche and explore partnership opportunities that align with your brand values.\n\n10. Monitor Analytics and Adapt:\nKeep a close eye on Instagram Insights to analyze the performance of your posts, identify the best times to post, and understand your audience demographics. Use this data to adapt your content strategy and optimize your Instagram blog for better engagement and growth.\n\nStarting an Instagram blog may seem overwhelming, but with passion, dedication, and a well-thought-out approach, success is within reach. Remember, it's not just about followers and likes; it's about creating meaningful connections and sharing your unique voice with the world. So, take the leap, stay true to yourself, and watch your Instagram blog thrive!`
    16→
    17→    },
    18→    {
    19→        title: 'What to Post on Instagram',
    20→        href: '/blog/what-to-post-on-instagram',
    21→        description:
    22→            'There are many different types of content that you can post on Instagram to engage with your followers and grow your following.',
    23→        date: 'Mar 10, 2023',
    24→        datetime: '2023-03-10',
    25→        imageUrl:
    26→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1611162616305-c69b3fa7fbe0?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=1674&q=80',
    27→        readingTime: '4 min',
    28→        author: {
    29→            name: 'Brenna Goyette',
    30→            imageUrl:
    31→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1550525811-e5869dd03032?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
    32→        },
    33→        blogPost: `
    34→Instagram is a powerful platform for engaging with your audience, but knowing what to post can sometimes be challenging. To help you keep your feed interesting and your followers engaged, we've put together a list of content ideas that you can use to make your Instagram shine!\n\n
    35→1. Behind-the-Scenes:\n
    36→Take your followers behind the scenes and show them the process of creating your products or services. People love to see the human side of your brand and get a glimpse of what goes on behind closed doors.\n\n
    37→2. User-Generated Content (UGC):\n
    38→Encourage your followers to create content featuring your products or services and share it on their own profiles. UGC not only gives your brand credibility but also helps foster a sense of community among your followers.\n\n
    39→3. Inspirational Quotes:\n
    40→Share motivational and inspirational quotes that align with your brand values. Quotes are highly shareable and can resonate with your audience on a personal level.\n\n
    41→4. Product or Service Highlights:\n
    42→Highlight your best-selling products or most popular services. Showcase their features and benefits to entice your audience to make a purchase.\n\n
    43→5. Tutorials and How-Tos:\n
    44→Educate your followers with step-by-step tutorials and how-to guides related to your industry. Providing value through educational content can establish your brand as an authority in your niche.\n\n
    45→6. Customer Testimonials:\n
    46→Share positive feedback and testimonials from satisfied customers. Testimonials build trust and social proof, which can encourage others to do business with you.\n\n
    47→7. Polls and Surveys:\n
    48→Engage your audience by running polls and surveys in your Instagram Stories. This not only encourages interaction but also helps you gather valuable insights from your followers.\n\n
    49→8. Throwback Posts:\n
    50→Take a trip down memory lane and share old photos or milestones from your brand's history. Nostalgic content can evoke positive emotions and create a sense of nostalgia among your audience.\n\n
    51→9. Seasonal Content:\n
    52→Tailor your content to fit holidays, seasons, or special occasions. Seasonal posts show that your brand is current and relevant.\n\n
    53→10. Fun Facts and Trivia:\n
    54→Share interesting and fun facts related to your industry or niche. Fun and quirky content can entertain your audience and keep them coming back for more.\n\n
    55→11. Collaborations and Partnerships:\n
    56→Highlight any collaborations or partnerships you have with other brands or influencers. Collaborative content can introduce your brand to a new audience and increase your reach.\n\n
    57→12. Company Culture:\n
    58→Give your followers a glimpse into your company culture and values. Share team photos, company events, and charitable initiatives.\n\n
    59→Remember, the key to a successful Instagram feed is to mix up your content and keep it engaging. Experiment with different types of posts and see what resonates best with your audience. Happy posting!`
    60→    },
    61→    {
    62→        title: 'Everything You Need To Know About Instagram Creator Studio',
    63→        href: '/blog/wverything-you-need-to-know-about-instagram-creator-studio',
    64→
    65→        description:
    66→            'Instagram Creator Studio is a tool that allows creators and businesses to manage their Instagram content and account from a desktop computer.',
    67→        date: 'Feb 12, 2023',
    68→        datetime: '2023-02-12',
    69→        imageUrl:
    70→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1505322747495-6afdd3b70760?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=1770&q=80',
    71→        readingTime: '11 min',
    72→        author: {
    73→            name: 'Daniela Metz',
    74→            imageUrl:
    75→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1487412720507-e7ab37603c6f?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
    76→        },
    77→        blogPost: `Instagram Creator Studio is a powerful tool that allows creators and businesses to manage their Instagram content and account from a desktop computer. If you want to optimize your Instagram presence and streamline your content creation process, here's everything you need to know about Instagram Creator Studio:\n\n
    78→1. Overview of Features:\n
    79→Instagram Creator Studio offers a range of features to help you manage and analyze your content. You can schedule and publish posts, view post insights, manage comments and direct messages, and track audience demographics.\n\n
    80→2. Content Scheduling:\n
    81→One of the key benefits of Instagram Creator Studio is the ability to schedule your posts in advance. This feature allows you to plan and organize your content calendar, ensuring a consistent posting schedule.\n\n
    82→3. Insights and Analytics:\n
    83→Understanding your audience and post performance is crucial for Instagram success. Instagram Creator Studio provides detailed insights and analytics, allowing you to track the reach, engagement, and impressions of your posts.\n\n
    84→4. Post Management:\n
    85→Easily manage your published posts through Instagram Creator Studio. You can edit captions, add location tags, and even delete or archive posts directly from your desktop.\n\n
    86→5. Content Library:\n
    87→The content library feature enables you to organize your media, making it easier to access and reuse your photos and videos for future posts.\n\n
    88→6. Direct Messaging:\n
    89→Stay connected with your followers by managing your Instagram direct messages through Creator Studio. Respond to messages and engage with your audience efficiently.\n\n
    90→7. Monetization Features:\n
    91→For eligible creators, Instagram Creator Studio provides access to monetization features such as IGTV ads and brand partnerships. This opens up opportunities for generating revenue through your Instagram content.\n\n
    92→8. Video Publishing:\n
    93→Creator Studio also supports video publishing, making it convenient to upload, schedule, and manage your IGTV videos directly from your desktop.\n\n
    94→9. Cross-Platform Management:\n
    95→In addition to Instagram, Creator Studio also allows you to manage content for your Facebook Pages. This integrated platform simplifies content management across both social media platforms.\n\n
    96→10. Accessible for Instagram Business and Creator Accounts:\n
    97→Instagram Creator Studio is available for both business accounts and creator accounts. If you have an Instagram business or creator account, you can access Creator Studio for free.\n\n
    98→In conclusion, Instagram Creator Studio is a valuable tool for Instagram content creators and businesses alike. Its range of features and user-friendly interface make it a must-have for anyone looking to enhance their Instagram presence. Take advantage of Instagram Creator Studio's capabilities and elevate your Instagram strategy to new heights!`
    99→
   100→    },
   101→    {
   102→        title: '20 Tactful Instagram Tips',
   103→        href: '/blog/20-tactful-instagram-tips',
   104→
   105→        description:
   106→            'Here is a collection of 20 tactful Instagram tips that will helps you to grow engagement, increase authentic followers, create quality content and so on',
   107→        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
   108→        datetime: '2023-03-16',
   109→        imageUrl:
   110→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1539187577537-e54cf54ae2f8?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80',
   111→        readingTime: '5 min',
   112→        author: {
   113→            name: 'Daniela Metz',
   114→            imageUrl:
   115→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1487412720507-e7ab37603c6f?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
   116→        },
   117→        blogPost: `Looking to up your Instagram game and boost your engagement? Here are 20 tactful Instagram tips to help you create a compelling and successful presence on the platform:\n\n
   118→1. Define Your Audience:\n
   119→Understand your target audience and create content that resonates with their interests and preferences.\n\n
   120→2. Showcase Your Personality:\n
   121→Let your personality shine through your posts and stories to connect with your followers on a personal level.\n\n
   122→3. Use High-Quality Images:\n
   123→Invest in high-quality photography or use professional editing apps to enhance the visual appeal of your posts.\n\n
   124→4. Craft Engaging Captions:\n
   125→Write captivating captions that complement your images and encourage your followers to interact with your content.\n\n
   126→5. Leverage Hashtags:\n
   127→Use relevant and trending hashtags to expand your reach and attract new followers.\n\n
   128→6. Engage with Your Audience:\n
   129→Respond to comments, direct messages, and mentions to foster a sense of community and build meaningful relationships.\n\n
   130→7. Collaborate with Others:\n
   131→Team up with other creators or brands for collaborations and cross-promotion to reach new audiences.\n\n
   132→8. Utilize Instagram Stories:\n
   133→Take advantage of Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and daily updates.\n\n
   134→9. Post Consistently:\n
   135→Maintain a consistent posting schedule to keep your audience engaged and coming back for more.\n\n
   136→10. Analyze Your Insights:\n
   137→Regularly check your Instagram Insights to understand what content performs best and tailor your strategy accordingly.\n\n
   138→11. Showcase User-Generated Content (UGC):\n
   139→Feature UGC from your followers to foster a sense of community and encourage others to create content related to your brand.\n\n
   140→12. Host Giveaways and Contests:\n
   141→Organize fun giveaways and contests to incentivize engagement and attract new followers.\n\n
   142→13. Share Behind-the-Scenes:\n
   143→Offer a glimpse into your creative process or daily life to make your content more relatable and authentic.\n\n
   144→14. Use Instagram Reels and IGTV:\n
   145→Explore Instagram Reels and IGTV for creating entertaining and longer-form content to diversify your feed.\n\n
   146→15. Optimize Your Bio:\n
   147→Craft a concise and impactful bio that clearly defines your brand and what you offer to your followers.\n\n
   148→16. Monitor Your Competitors:\n
   149→Keep an eye on your competitors' strategies to stay informed about industry trends and stay ahead.\n\n
   150→17. Follow Instagram Trends:\n
   151→Stay updated on the latest Instagram features and trends to keep your content fresh and relevant.\n\n
   152→18. Use Instagram Shopping:\n
   153→Leverage Instagram Shopping to tag products in your posts and stories for a seamless shopping experience.\n\n
   154→19. Track Link Clicks:\n
   155→Use link tracking tools to monitor the performance of links in your bio or swipe-up links in Stories.\n\n
   156→20. Stay Authentic and Transparent:\n
   157→Be true to yourself and your brand values. Authenticity builds trust and loyalty among your followers.\n\n
   158→By implementing these tactful Instagram tips, you can elevate your Instagram presence, foster meaningful connections, and create a vibrant and engaged community of followers. Happy Instagramming!`
   159→

   176→        },
   177→        blogPost: `If you're a business looking to harness the power of Instagram for marketing, you're in the right place. Here are some essential Instagram marketing resources to help you build a strong presence and achieve your business goals:\n\n

   199→Curate and organize your Stories Highlights to showcase your best content and products.\n\n

   203→Run contests and giveaways to engage your audience and drive excitement around your brand.\n\n
   204→14. Instagram Live:\n
   205→Go live on Instagram to connect with your followers in real-time and host Q&A sessions or product launches.\n\n
   206→15. Instagram Insights API:\n
   207→Leverage the Insights API to access more in-depth data and integrate it into your existing analytics tools.\n\n
   208→16. Instagram Reels for Business:\n
   209→Create entertaining and creative Reels to capture your audience's attention and showcase your products.\n\n
   210→17. Instagram Guides:\n
   211→Use Guides to create valuable and informative content that provides value to your audience.\n\n
   212→18. Instagram Engagement Strategies:\n
   213→Develop strategies to encourage engagement, such as asking questions, running polls, and responding to comments.\n\n

   220→    {
   221→        title: 'How to run an Instagram Contest?',
   222→        href: '/blog/how-to-run-an-instagram-contest',
   223→        description:
   224→            'An Instagram contest is a promotional activity where users are asked to submit content (e.g. a photo, video, or text) or perform an action (e.g. like, comment, or share) in exchange for a chance to win a prize',
   225→        date: 'Mar 10, 2023',
   226→        datetime: '2023-03-10',
   227→        imageUrl:
   228→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1562147458-0c12e8d29f50?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=1770&q=80',
   229→        readingTime: '4 min',
   230→        author: {
   231→            name: 'Roel Aufderehar',
   232→            imageUrl:
   233→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
   234→        }, blogPost: `Running an Instagram contest can be an exciting way to engage your audience and boost brand awareness. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to run a successful Instagram contest:\n\n
   235→        1. Define Your Goals:\n
   236→        Determine the purpose of your contest. Are you looking to increase followers, generate user-generated content (UGC), or promote a new product?\n\n
   237→        2. Choose the Contest Type:\n
   238→        Decide on the type of contest that aligns with your goals. Popular options include like-to-win, photo contests, and hashtag challenges.\n\n
   239→        3. Set Clear Rules and Guidelines:\n
   240→        Establish straightforward and easy-to-understand rules for participation, eligibility, and prize distribution.\n\n
   241→        4. Select a Relevant Prize:\n
   242→        Offer a prize that resonates with your target audience and encourages them to participate.\n\n
   243→        5. Create Eye-Catching Graphics:\n
   244→        Design visually appealing graphics and promotional materials to announce the contest.\n\n
   245→        6. Decide on the Contest Duration:\n
   246→        Choose a specific start and end date for the contest to create a sense of urgency.\n\n
   247→        7. Promote the Contest:\n
   248→        Use your social media channels, email list, and website to promote the contest and reach a wider audience.\n\n
   249→        8. Monitor Entries and Engage:\n
   250→        Keep track of contest entries, interact with participants, and respond to questions or concerns.\n\n
   251→        9. Choose a Fair Winner:\n
   252→        Select the contest winner fairly and transparently based on the stated rules.\n\n
   253→        10. Announce the Winner:\n
   254→        Publicly announce the winner and thank all participants for their involvement.\n\n
   255→        11. Follow Legal and Instagram Guidelines:\n
   256→        Ensure your contest complies with all relevant laws and Instagram's promotion guidelines.\n\n
   257→        12. Measure Results:\n
   258→        Analyze the success of the contest based on your initial goals and use the insights to improve future contests.\n\n
   259→        13. Repurpose UGC:\n
   260→        Leverage the user-generated content from the contest to showcase your brand and products.\n\n
   261→        14. Express Gratitude:\n
   262→        Show appreciation to your audience for participating and supporting your brand.\n\n
   263→        Running an Instagram contest can not only drive engagement but also foster a sense of community around your brand. By following these steps, you can create a fun and rewarding experience for your followers and achieve your marketing objectives.`
   264→

   325→    {
   326→        title: 'How to Promote Your eCommerce Store on Instagram',

   328→        description:
   329→            'There are many ways to promote your store like optimizing your profile, using keywords in your bio and making your profile set to public.',
   330→        date: 'Mar 10, 2023',
   331→        datetime: '2023-03-10',
   332→        imageUrl:
   333→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1564758596018-3e5b1f2340cc?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=928&q=80',
   334→        readingTime: '4 min',
   335→        author: {
   336→            name: 'Roel Aufderehar',
   337→            imageUrl:
   338→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
   339→        },
   340→        blogPost: `Promoting your eCommerce store on Instagram can significantly boost your online presence and drive sales. If you're looking to leverage Instagram to grow your eCommerce business, here are some effective strategies to get started:\n\n
   341→1. Optimize Your Instagram Profile:\n
   342→Ensure your Instagram bio clearly communicates your brand, products, and value proposition. Use a recognizable profile picture and include a link to your eCommerce store.\n\n
   343→2. Create High-Quality Visuals:\n
   344→Post eye-catching product photos and lifestyle images that showcase your products in the best light. Use professional photography or high-quality graphics to make your content stand out.\n\n
   345→3. Use Product Tags and Shoppable Posts:\n
   346→Take advantage of Instagram's product tags and shoppable posts features to make it easy for users to shop directly from your posts.\n\n
   347→4. Run Instagram Ads:\n
   348→Invest in Instagram ads to reach a wider audience and promote your best-selling products or special offers.\n\n
   349→5. Host Giveaways and Contests:\n
   350→Organize giveaways and contests to create buzz around your eCommerce store and attract new followers and potential customers.\n\n
   351→6. Collaborate with Influencers:\n
   352→Partner with influencers in your niche to showcase your products to their engaged audience and gain credibility.\n\n
   353→7. Utilize Instagram Stories and Highlights:\n
   354→Use Instagram Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, product demonstrations, and limited-time offers. Save important Stories as Highlights on your profile for easy access.\n\n
   355→8. Offer Exclusive Promotions:\n
   356→Provide exclusive discounts or promotions to your Instagram followers to incentivize them to make a purchase.\n\n
   357→9. Engage with Your Audience:\n
   358→Respond to comments, direct messages, and engage with your followers to build a loyal customer base.\n\n
   359→10. Collaborate with Other Brands:\n
   360→Partner with complementary brands for co-marketing initiatives or joint promotions to expand your reach.\n\n
   361→11. Showcase User-Generated Content (UGC):\n
   362→Repost and feature user-generated content that showcases customers using and enjoying your products.\n\n
   363→12. Leverage Instagram Guides and Reels:\n
   364→Create helpful product guides or entertaining Reels that highlight your products and brand personality.\n\n
   365→13. Utilize Instagram Shopping Features:\n
   366→Make use of Instagram Shopping features like the Shop tab on your profile and the Shopping Explore page to increase product visibility.\n\n
   367→14. Promote Seasonal and Holiday Offers:\n
   368→Plan and promote seasonal sales and holiday offers to capitalize on festive shopping trends.\n\n
   369→15. Monitor and Analyze Performance:\n
   370→Regularly review Instagram Insights and track the performance of your posts and campaigns to refine your strategies.\n\n
   371→16. Showcase Customer Reviews and Testimonials:\n
   372→Feature positive customer reviews and testimonials to build trust and social proof.\n\n
   373→17. Use Instagram Live:\n
   374→Host live sessions to showcase new products, conduct Q&A sessions, or share valuable tips with your audience.\n\n
   375→18. Create Shareable Content:\n
   376→Design shareable graphics or videos that resonate with your target audience and encourage them to share your content.\n\n
   377→19. Run Instagram Giveaway Ads:\n
   378→Use Instagram giveaway ads to reach a wider audience and attract participants to your promotions.\n\n
   379→20. Measure ROI and Adjust Strategies:\n
   380→Continuously measure the ROI of your Instagram efforts and adjust your promotional strategies based on the data to optimize results.\n\n
   381→By implementing these Instagram marketing strategies, you can effectively promote your eCommerce store and drive meaningful engagement and conversions.`

   383→    {
   384→        title: 'A Beginner’s Guide to Instagram for Businesses',
   385→        href: '/blog/how-to-use-instagram-for-business-a-beginners-guide/',
   386→
   387→        description:
   388→            'Instagram for business is a way for companies to promote their products or services on the popular social media platform.',
   389→        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
   390→        datetime: '2023-03-16',
   391→        imageUrl:
   392→            'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1598128558393-70ff21433be0?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=1089&q=80',
   393→        readingTime: '5 min',
   394→        author: {
   395→            name: 'Daniela Metz',
   396→            imageUrl:
   397→                'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1487412720507-e7ab37603c6f?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
   398→        },
   399→        blogPost: `If you're a business looking to harness the power of Instagram for growth and success, this beginner's guide is for you. Instagram is a valuable platform for businesses to connect with their target audience, build brand awareness, and drive sales. Here's how to get started with Instagram for business:\n\n
   400→1. Set Up an Instagram Business Account:\n
   401→Create a dedicated business account on Instagram. It provides access to valuable insights and features like Instagram Ads and Shopping.\n\n
   402→2. Optimize Your Profile:\n
   403→Use your business name as your username and include a recognizable profile picture, preferably your logo. Write a compelling bio that clearly communicates your brand identity and value proposition.\n\n
   404→3. Define Your Target Audience:\n
   405→Identify your target audience and understand their preferences, interests, and pain points. This knowledge will guide your content strategy.\n\n
   406→4. Create High-Quality Visual Content:\n
   407→Invest in creating visually appealing and high-quality images and videos that represent your brand. Use professional photography and graphic design to make your content stand out.\n\n
   408→5. Develop a Content Strategy:\n
   409→Plan your content in advance to ensure consistency and relevance. Mix promotional content with valuable and entertaining posts to keep your audience engaged.\n\n
   410→6. Use Hashtags Strategically:\n
   411→Research and use relevant hashtags to reach a wider audience and increase discoverability. Create a branded hashtag to encourage user-generated content (UGC).\n\n
   412→7. Engage with Your Audience:\n
   413→Respond to comments, messages, and mentions promptly. Engage with your audience through likes, comments, and reposts of UGC.\n\n
   414→8. Utilize Instagram Stories and Highlights:\n
   415→Leverage Instagram Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, product launches, and limited-time offers. Save important Stories as Highlights on your profile to showcase them permanently.\n\n
   416→9. Collaborate with Influencers and Partners:\n
   417→Partner with influencers and complementary businesses to extend your reach and credibility.\n\n
   418→10. Run Instagram Contests and Giveaways:\n
   419→Organize contests and giveaways to boost engagement and attract new followers.\n\n
   420→11. Measure Performance with Instagram Insights:\n
   421→Regularly review Instagram Insights to understand your audience demographics, post reach, and engagement. Use these insights to refine your strategies.\n\n
   422→12. Utilize Instagram Ads:\n
   423→Invest in Instagram Ads to reach a broader audience and promote your products or services effectively.\n\n
   424→13. Monitor Your Competitors:\n
   425→Keep an eye on your competitors' Instagram activities to gain insights into what works and what doesn't in your industry.\n\n
   426→14. Leverage Instagram Shopping Features:\n
   427→Use Instagram Shopping to tag products in your posts and Stories, making it easy for users to shop directly from your content.\n\n
   428→15. Showcase Customer Testimonials:\n
   429→Feature positive customer reviews and testimonials to build trust and credibility.\n\n
   430→16. Analyze and Adjust Your Strategy:\n
   431→Regularly analyze the performance of your Instagram efforts and adjust your strategy based on the results.\n\n
   432→By following these tips and best practices, you'll be well on your way to establishing a strong presence on Instagram and driving business growth. Remember, consistency and authenticity are key to success on this vibrant social platform.`

---

## Summary of Key Patterns

- **Component Composition:** Heavy use of child component composition with prop drilling
- **State Management:** Functional components with React hooks (useState, useEffect, useRef)
- **Form Handling:** Email validation with regex, conditional loading/success states
- **Authentication:** Props-based auth state (loggedIn boolean, setter functions)
- **Routing:** React Router (Link, useNavigate)
- **Styling:** Tailwind CSS with dark theme (gray-900 background, indigo/pink accents)
- **Data:** Imported from data files (blogPostsData, pricingTiers, etc.)
- **Third-party:** ReCAPTCHA integration, Headless UI Disclosure component

content.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the app. Let me plan my approach:

1. **Install msw-storybook-addon** - needed for network mocking
2. **Set up preview.tsx** with:
   - Import root CSS (`../src/core-ui/index.css`)
   - BrowserRouter decorator (React Router is used everywhere)
   - MSW initialization
   - sessionStorage seeding for login state
3. **Create MSW handlers** for Airtable API and ReCAPTCHA
4. **Write ~10 story files** for meaningful components


5. **Start with infrastructure setup** - installing the addon and configuring preview.tsx with the necessary decorators and mocks

I'm noticing the app relies heavily on prop drilling for auth state, uses sessionStorage for persistence, and has several key dependencies like moment for dates and Headlessui for disclosure components. I'll need to target components like Navigation, Footer, and Landing as my first stories, making sure to mock the Airtable API calls and handle the ReCAPTCHA integration properly.

The remaining pages (Blog, Contact, Pricing, Faq, About, Jobs) and sub-components like Hero, Team, and Feature will follow once the core infrastructure is in place. BlogPost is particularly interesting since it reads from window.location.pathname directly, so I'll need to handle that routing behavior in the story setup. I'm narrowing down which components would make the best Storybook stories—I want a mix of low-level reusable pieces like Navigation and Footer, some mid-level components with interesting behavior like Hero and Team, and then the full pages like Landing, Blog, Contact, and Pricing to showcase complete compositions. I'm realizing some of these components don't have enough meaningful variants to justify separate stories. Let me consolidate and focus on components that actually have interesting state differences—Navigation with logged-out and logged-in states, Landing with the same variants, and Pricing too. For the rest like Hero, Newsletter, Blog, and Contact, I'll stick with single default stories since the variations aren't substantial enough to warrant multiple versions.

Now I need to set up the Storybook infrastructure. The preview file will handle importing styles, wrapping stories with BrowserRouter, and configuring MSW for API mocking along with session cleanup between tests. But I'm hitting a problem with the MSW handlers—they're trying to use an environment variable for the Airtable server URL that won't be available in the Storybook environment, so those fetch requests will fail with an undefined endpoint.

Actually, looking at where the fetches actually happen, the App component itself makes the Airtable calls, but I'm not rendering App in the stories. The individual page components that do make network requests are the form components—SignInForm, SignUpForm, and ContactForm all use ReCAPTCHA verification. The page components themselves like Landing, Blog, Contact, Pricing, and the others don't fetch data directly. So I mainly need to mock out the ReCAPTCHA requests and handle any newsletter signup calls, while the Hero component's email form just validates locally without making a fetch.

Let me double-check the Hero, Newsletter, and JobsForm components to see if they're actually making network calls or just managing form state with loading indicators. I need to verify whether they're calling fetch before I set up the mocks.

For the story setup, I'll need BrowserRouter for the navigation components, the root CSS import, and MSW to handle any API calls that do occur. I should also check if msw-storybook-addon is already in the dependencies.

Looking at the package.json, I don't see MSW installed, so I'll need to add both msw and msw-storybook-addon. I should also check for a public directory for static assets before I start writing the stories.

Let me begin by installing the dependencies, setting up the preview configuration, creating the MSW handlers, and then writing out the story files. For the ReCAPTCHA components, I'll need to handle the fact that they load external scripts which might not work properly in Storybook.

Actually, I'm reconsidering which components to target for stories. The requirements ask for low-level reusable components, browser-state-backed components, fetched-data components, and page components. So I should focus on Navigation and Footer as reusable pieces, Navigation again for the browser state aspect since it reads the loggedIn state, and then the various pages like Landing, Blog, Contact, Pricing, FAQ, About, and Jobs. The fetched-data requirement is mostly handled at the App level and passed down as props, so I might need to think about how to demonstrate that pattern.

I'm settling on ten story files that cover these requirements: Navigation with LoggedOut and LoggedIn variants, Footer, Hero, Team, Landing with variants, Blog, Contact, Pricing with variants, FAQ, and Jobs.

Since several components rely on `Link` from react-router-dom and Navigation uses `useNavigate`, I need a router wrapper in the Storybook decorator. I'm going with `MemoryRouter` instead of `BrowserRouter` because it's the standard Storybook approach—it avoids URL side effects in the iframe environment while still supporting the routing patterns the real app uses.

Now I'm checking how images are handled across the components. Local assets in `src/assets/` are imported as modules and handled by Vite, but some components pull external image URLs from data objects like blog posts and team members. I'll proceed with the implementation and let external images load naturally—if they're unreachable, I can add MSW mocking later if needed.

Before installing MSW, let me figure out which components actually make network requests. Looking through the forms—Hero, Newsletter, and JobsForm all have email submissions, but I need to verify if they're actually fetching or just managing local state.

The Contact page is trickier since it uses ReCAPTCHA, which will try to load Google's script and could cause issues in Storybook. I'll proceed with the MSW setup as instructed and handle any network-related problems as they come up. Before diving in, I should check a few things—whether there's a public directory, what the Hero component actually does, and how ContactForm works. Looking back at the exploration report, Hero has an email input with loading and success states, so it likely handles form submission. Let me start reading those files and begin the implementation in parallel. Setting up the Storybook preview configuration with routing and styling...

Now I'm reconsidering whether to include MSW since most of my target components don't actually make network requests—the forms that do submit to APIs can be handled differently during testing. I need to set up MSW with `onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass'` so unhandled requests pass through normally, since the forms only fetch on submit and I'm not rendering the App component that would fetch on mount. ReCAPTCHA script loading won't be intercepted by MSW anyway, so I'll install the addon and start without specific handlers, adding them only if tests fail.

Let me read through Hero.jsx and a couple other components to see what fetch calls they're making, then install the dependencies and start writing the Storybook files.

- Navigation.jsx: Needs `loggedIn`, `setLoggedIn`, `setTriggeredLogout` props, uses `useNavigate`
- Footer.jsx: No props, uses `NavLink`


- BlogPost relies on matching window.location.pathname against blog post data, which won't work in Storybook, so I'll skip that one and focus on other components instead.

Now I'm reviewing the remaining components I need to cover: the Pricing page in both its landing and route versions, Contact components, and FaqList which uses Headless UI's Disclosure component. I should also take a closer look at the Profile component since it has more complex prop requirements.

content.thinking:
Now I have everything I need. Let me also read a couple more files - the NotFound component and the FaqList component that uses Headless UI Disclosure.

Actually, I already have all the information I need. Let me now write all the files.

Let me also look at the Faq component imports again: `FaqList` is imported from `./FaqList`, which is at `src/routes/faq/FaqList.jsx`. The exploration noted it uses `@headlessui/react` Disclosure.

Now let me write everything:

1. **Update .storybook/main.ts** - add staticDirs for public/


2. **Update .storybook/preview.tsx** - add CSS import, MemoryRouter decorator, MSW setup
3. **Create .storybook/msw-handlers.ts** - empty handlers for now
4. **Write 10 story files**

Looking at my components, none of them actually make network requests on mount - Hero, Newsletter, JobsForm, Navigation, Footer all work with local data, and the page components are just compositions of these. Feature and Team pull from imported files. The only external requests would be for images, so I can keep the MSW handlers minimal for now.

Since the instructions require MSW, I'll configure it with `onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass'` to let external image URLs pass through normally. Now I'm ready to start writing the stories. I'll set up the MSW infrastructure with an empty handlers file to satisfy the addon requirement, even though none of my story components actually call external APIs.

Setting up Storybook configuration...

Now I'm creating story files for the main components—Navigation, Footer, Hero, Feature, Team, and Landing—to ensure they render correctly in isolation with the proper setup.

I need to double-check the component names for the remaining routes. For the Faq component, it's exported as `FaqPage`, so I'll import it that way in the story file to match the actual export.

Now I'm thinking through the Navigation component stories—it requires `loggedIn`, `setLoggedIn`, and `setTriggeredLogout` props. I need to mock these functions using Storybook's `fn()` utility, which should be available in the current version. I'll follow the pattern from the instructions and use `render` functions with JSX to define the stories for both Navigation and Landing components, mirroring how they're actually used in the app.

Now I'm setting up stories for the remaining components. Most of them are straightforward with just a Default story that renders the component as-is, but Pricing needs both a logged-out and logged-in variant like Navigation and Landing do.

Looking at the components more carefully, I realize most of these don't have meaningful variants to showcase. Blog, Hero, Feature, Team, and Footer are all data-driven or have internal state management, so a single Default story captures their essence. That's perfectly fine—not every component needs multiple stories just to hit a target number. so I need around 30 total stories across the files. Looking at what I have, most components genuinely only have one meaningful state, which is fine—the guideline is flexible depending on whether the component actually has multiple states worth documenting. Let me think about whether I should include some of the other components like NotFound or Testimonials, or if I should focus on expanding the ones that do have multiple states like Comparison, which takes a loggedIn prop and has responsive behavior I haven't fully captured yet.

Actually, I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just map out the real patterns I'm covering: low-level reusables like Navigation and Footer, plus full pages like Landing and Pricing. That gives me about 10 files with 12 stories, which feels like reasonable coverage without forcing artificial variants. I could add a mobile viewport to Navigation, but that's not really a different story—it's the same component at a different size. Same issue with testing form states in Hero; those are internal states, not meaningful variants. Maybe I should look for other components that actually have multiple meaningful states instead of stretching the ones I have.

Looking at what else is available, I see several components with loggedIn variants—Cta, Comparison, and PricingComparisonTable—which are genuinely different states worth testing. There are also single-state components like NotFound, Stats, Partners, Testimonials, Newsletter, and others. I could swap out some of the single-story components for ones that have real state variations.

Let me consolidate this into 10 files that give me good coverage. I'd include Navigation and Landing with their loggedIn/loggedOut variants, Pricing with both states, and then fill in the rest with Footer, Hero, Feature, Team, Blog, Faq, and About. Jobs is tempting too since it has both JobsListing and JobsForm, so maybe I keep both About and Jobs instead. I'm reconsidering whether to keep Feature as a standalone story since it's a good isolated example of a data-driven component, and maybe swap out one of the pages for NotFound to showcase that unique pattern. Let me just commit to this final list of 10 files and move forward with the implementation.

Before I start writing, I need to verify that the Faq component uses Headless UI's Disclosure component, which should work fine in Storybook as a standard npm dependency. The component exports FaqPage as the default, so I'll reference that directly in the story configuration. I'm using .jsx extensions consistently across all story files. I'm torn between using `.tsx` for type safety with Meta and StoryObj versus sticking with `.jsx` to match the existing component files, but I need to check the tsconfig to see if TypeScript will actually accept importing untyped JSX components.

     5→        href: "/blog/how-to-start-an-instagram-blog",
     6→        description: "Starting an Instagram blog is relatively easy and can be done in a few simple steps and some creativity as well",
     7→        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
     8→        datetime: "2023-03-16",
     9→        imageUrl: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1600096194534-95cf5ece04cf?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80",

    12→            name: 'Roel Aufderehar',
    13→            imageUrl: 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1472099645785-5658abf4ff4e?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=eyJhcHBfaWQiOjEyMDd9&auto=format&fit=facearea&facepad=2&w=256&h=256&q=80',
    14→        },
    15→        blogPost: `In today's digital age, social media has become an integral part of our lives, and Instagram, with its visually captivating platform, stands out as a popular choice for content creators looking to start a blog. Whether you're passionate about fashion, travel, food, fitness, or any other niche, Instagram offers an excellent opportunity to showcase your creativity and engage with a global audience. So, if you're eager to share your passions with the world, here's a step-by-step guide on how to start an Instagram blog and make it a resounding success.

1. Define Your Niche:
Before diving headfirst into Instagram blogging, it's crucial to identify your niche. Determine what interests you the most and where your expertise lies. A well-defined niche helps you stand out in a sea of content creators and attracts a dedicated audience genuinely interested in your content.

2. Create a Compelling Instagram Handle:
Your Instagram handle is essentially your brand name, so make it catchy and reflective of your niche. Keep it simple, memorable, and easy to spell to ensure that potential followers can find and remember you easily.

3. Develop a Cohesive Aesthetic:
Instagram is a visual platform, and maintaining a consistent and attractive aesthetic is key to grabbing attention and retaining followers. Choose a color palette, editing style, and overall theme that aligns with your niche and reflects your personality.

4. Craft an Engaging Bio:
Your Instagram bio is your chance to make a great first impression. Use it to concisely describe who you are, what your blog is about, and what value you provide to your followers. Include relevant keywords and a clear call-to-action (CTA) to encourage visitors to follow you.

5. Share High-Quality Content:
Quality is paramount on Instagram. Invest in a good camera or use your smartphone's advanced camera features to capture stunning images. Ensure your photos are well-lit, well-composed, and visually appealing. Additionally, use engaging captions that provide context, ask questions, or encourage interaction.

6. Plan Your Content Strategy:
Consistency is key to Instagram success. Plan your content in advance using content calendars or scheduling tools. This will help you maintain a steady flow of posts and ensure that you cover a diverse range of topics within your niche.

7. Utilize Instagram Stories and IGTV:
Instagram Stories and IGTV are powerful tools for connecting with your audience in a more authentic and engaging way. Use Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and announcements. IGTV allows for longer-form content, giving you an opportunity to showcase in-depth tutorials, travel vlogs, or interviews.

8. Engage with Your Audience:
Interact with your followers regularly by responding to comments, answering direct messages, and acknowledging user-generated content (UGC). Building a strong relationship with your audience fosters loyalty and encourages them to support your blog in the long run.

9. Collaborate and Network:
Collaborations with other content creators, brands, or influencers can significantly boost your exposure. Look for like-minded individuals or companies within your niche and explore partnership opportunities that align with your brand values.

10. Monitor Analytics and Adapt:
Keep a close eye on Instagram Insights to analyze the performance of your posts, identify the best times to post, and understand your audience demographics. Use this data to adapt your content strategy and optimize your Instagram blog for better engagement and growth.

Starting an Instagram blog may seem overwhelming, but with passion, dedication, and a well-thought-out approach, success is within reach. Remember, it's not just about followers and likes; it's about creating meaningful connections and sharing your unique voice with the world. So, take the leap, stay true to yourself, and watch your Instagram blog thrive!`

    33→        blogPost: `
    34→Instagram is a powerful platform for engaging with your audience, but knowing what to post can sometimes be challenging. To help you keep your feed interesting and your followers engaged, we've put together a list of content ideas that you can use to make your Instagram shine!


    35→1. Behind-the-Scenes:

    36→Take your followers behind the scenes and show them the process of creating your products or services. People love to see the human side of your brand and get a glimpse of what goes on behind closed doors.


    37→2. User-Generated Content (UGC):

    38→Encourage your followers to create content featuring your products or services and share it on their own profiles. UGC not only gives your brand credibility but also helps foster a sense of community among your followers.


    39→3. Inspirational Quotes:

    40→Share motivational and inspirational quotes that align with your brand values. Quotes are highly shareable and can resonate with your audience on a personal level.


    41→4. Product or Service Highlights:

    42→Highlight your best-selling products or most popular services. Showcase their features and benefits to entice your audience to make a purchase.


    43→5. Tutorials and How-Tos:

    44→Educate your followers with step-by-step tutorials and how-to guides related to your industry. Providing value through educational content can establish your brand as an authority in your niche.


    45→6. Customer Testimonials:

    46→Share positive feedback and testimonials from satisfied customers. Testimonials build trust and social proof, which can encourage others to do business with you.


    47→7. Polls and Surveys:

    48→Engage your audience by running polls and surveys in your Instagram Stories. This not only encourages interaction but also helps you gather valuable insights from your followers.


    49→8. Throwback Posts:

    50→Take a trip down memory lane and share old photos or milestones from your brand's history. Nostalgic content can evoke positive emotions and create a sense of nostalgia among your audience.
